terms.definition: Spherical component of a spectacle prescription correcting myopia (negative) or hyperopia (positive).
terms.exampleSentence: The sphere was recorded as -2.25 DS in the right eye.
terms.contextNotes: Always specify sign and units in dioptres (D).

terms.definition: Cylindrical lens component correcting astigmatism, with both a power and an axis.
terms.exampleSentence: A cylinder of -1.00 DC at axis 90 corrected the with-the-rule astigmatism.
terms.contextNotes: UK convention is minus cylinder form.

terms.definition: Orientation in degrees (1-180) of the cylindrical correction in a spectacle prescription.
terms.exampleSentence: Rechecking the axis confirmed it lay at 175 degrees rather than 5.
terms.contextNotes: Express as a whole number in degrees.

terms.definition: Reading addition power added to the distance prescription for presbyopic correction.
terms.exampleSentence: An addition of +2.00 D supported comfortable near reading at 33 cm.
terms.contextNotes: Typical adds range +0.75 to +3.00 D depending on age and demand.

terms.definition: Optical element prescribed in prism dioptres to displace the image, used to manage diplopia and binocular imbalance.
terms.exampleSentence: Two prism dioptres base-out were dispensed to alleviate convergence insufficiency.
terms.contextNotes: Document magnitude (Δ) and base direction together.

terms.definition: Orientation of the thicker edge of a prism (base in/out/up/down) determining direction of image displacement.
terms.exampleSentence: Base direction was set base-down to compensate for the right hyperphoria.
terms.contextNotes: Use BI/BO/BU/BD abbreviations on prescriptions.

terms.definition: Distance in millimetres between the centres of the two pupils, used to align lenses with visual axes.
terms.exampleSentence: A pupillary distance of 64 mm was recorded on the spectacle order.
terms.contextNotes: Measure monocular PDs for progressive lenses.

terms.definition: Latin term for the right eye, abbreviated OD on prescriptions.
terms.contextNotes: Always list OD before OS by convention.

terms.definition: Latin term for the left eye, abbreviated OS on prescriptions.
terms.exampleSentence: OS visual acuity was reduced to 6/18 unaided.
terms.contextNotes: Some EHRs prefer 'L' and 'R' to avoid confusion.

terms.definition: Latin term meaning 'each eye' or 'both eyes', abbreviated OU on prescriptions.
terms.exampleSentence: Drop one drop of latanoprost OU at bedtime.
terms.contextNotes: Increasingly written 'BE' or 'both eyes' to reduce errors.

terms.definition: Process of selecting, assessing and dispensing contact lenses appropriate to the patient's eyes and lifestyle.
terms.exampleSentence: A contact lens fitting appointment was booked for the new keratoconus patient.
terms.contextNotes: Document base curve, diameter, power and brand.

terms.definition: Specialist evaluation of residual vision and prescribing of magnifiers, illumination and rehabilitation strategies.
terms.exampleSentence: Following the low vision assessment, a 4x stand magnifier was issued.
terms.contextNotes: Discuss CVI registration and local social services.

terms.definition: Technique of placing a single drop into the lower fornix while the patient looks up.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient was taught drop instillation by gently pulling down the lower lid.
terms.contextNotes: Teach punctal occlusion to reduce systemic absorption.

terms.definition: Cleaning, disinfection and storage routine essential to prevent contact lens-related microbial keratitis.
terms.exampleSentence: Poor contact lens hygiene was suspected as the cause of microbial keratitis.
terms.contextNotes: Never rinse lenses or cases with tap water.

terms.definition: Written communication referring a patient from optometry to a hospital eye service or consultant ophthalmologist.
terms.exampleSentence: An ophthalmology referral was sent for assessment of suspected wet AMD.
terms.contextNotes: Use NHS e-Referral Service where available.

terms.definition: Referral requiring assessment within days, used for sight-threatening or rapidly progressive conditions.
terms.exampleSentence: An urgent referral was made for sudden painless visual loss to exclude retinal artery occlusion.
terms.contextNotes: Phone the on-call team for any same-day acute referral.

terms.definition: Referral for phacoemulsification when lens opacity affects function and visual outcomes are likely to improve.
terms.exampleSentence: A cataract surgery referral was completed once acuity dropped below the local threshold.
terms.contextNotes: Document patient-reported impact, not just acuity, per NICE.

terms.definition: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines covering glaucoma, AMD, cataract and other eye conditions.
terms.exampleSentence: Treatment was escalated in line with NICE eye care guidance for chronic open-angle glaucoma.
terms.contextNotes: Cite specific guideline number in referral letters.

terms.definition: NHS-funded General Ophthalmic Services sight test available to eligible patients in the UK.
terms.exampleSentence: She qualified for a GOS sight test as a person with diabetes.
terms.contextNotes: Confirm eligibility before claiming the GOS fee.

terms.definition: Measure of the spatial resolving capacity of the visual system, recorded as Snellen or LogMAR.
terms.exampleSentence: Best-corrected visual acuity was 6/9 right and 6/6 left.
terms.contextNotes: Always document distance, correction and chart used.

terms.definition: Scheduled review of a patient to monitor progression, response to treatment or healing.
terms.exampleSentence: A follow-up appointment was arranged in three months to repeat the visual fields.
terms.contextNotes: Safety-net with clear advice on red flag symptoms.

terms.definition: Process of providing patients with sufficient information to make a voluntary decision about an investigation or treatment.
terms.exampleSentence: Informed consent for dilation was documented before instilling tropicamide.
terms.contextNotes: Mention risks, benefits, alternatives and the option to decline.

terms.definition: Clinical symptom that should prompt urgent or emergency assessment, such as sudden vision loss or new flashes.
terms.exampleSentence: Sudden onset diplopia was flagged as a red flag symptom needing same-day review.
terms.contextNotes: Always safety-net verbally and in writing.

terms.definition: Extent to which a patient uses prescribed eye drops correctly and on schedule.
terms.exampleSentence: Poor eye drop adherence was identified as the cause of inadequate IOP control.
terms.contextNotes: Consider once-daily regimens and adherence aids.

terms.definition: Written specification of refractive correction including sphere, cylinder, axis, ADD, prism and PD.
terms.exampleSentence: The spectacle prescription was issued and remains valid for two years.
terms.contextNotes: Patient is entitled to a copy under UK regulations.
